content.problemStatement:
In C, the not equal to (`!=`) operator is used to compare two values to check if they are not equal. If the values are different, it returns true (`1`), and if the values are the same, it returns false (`0`).

**Syntax:**
```c
value1 != value2
```
---
**Comparing Integers:**
```c
int num1 = 5;
int num2 = 10;
printf("%d\n", num1 != num2);  // Outputs: 1 (true) because 5 != 10
```

**Comparing Floating-Point Numbers:**
```c
double price1 = 9.99;
double price2 = 8.99;
printf("%d\n", price1 != price2);  // Outputs: 1 (true) because 9.99 != 8.99
```

**Comparing Float and Int:**
```c
float a = 7.5f;
int b = 7;
printf("%d\n", a != b);  // Outputs: 1 (true) because 7.5 != 7
```
